judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with well-grounded commit citations; they are comparable, with Report 2 slightly more thorough.

report_1.evidence: This exact migration (Cargobox → Edgerunner for this exact API) was already tried, shipped a stopgap fix, and was reverted after causing a production outage (`FATAL: too many connections` on `/reports/monthly`), per commits `454a21f`, `597777b`, and `1712565` in `carelog-api`.

report_2.evidence: `git log --oneline --all` in `carelog-api/` — shows this exact migration was already tried and reverted (commits `454a21f`, `597777b`, `1712565`, ticket PLAT-31).

report_1.evidence: opening a fresh Postgres connection per request instead of using a pool (`597777b`) — was tried in this codebase and caused `/reports/monthly` to exhaust Postgres's `max_connections` in production under "normal dashboard load," per the revert commit message

report_2.evidence: Commit `1712565`'s message states this migration was already deployed and failed: *"serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production."*

report_1.evidence: This repository already contains a complete (later-reverted) attempt at exactly this migration ... Per the project documentation guidance, that makes this a settled decision, not an open design question

report_2.evidence: a `backend/serverless_handler.py` wrapping the FastAPI `app`, and a `deploy/edgerunner.toml` with `[service] name/entry` and `[scaling] per_request = true`. These files don't exist on `main` today (removed in the revert)

report_1.evidence: the decision to re-attempt should not be made by an engineer alone ... I'd recommend involving: Whoever owns the PLAT-31 ticket / the original migration decision, since they have the incident context that may not be fully captured in the commit messages.

report_2.evidence: the Platform team appears to own both Cargobox and this migration path already and has direct institutional knowledge of why it failed — they should lead any new attempt.
